questions.question: What is the main purpose of a structured mock workflow?
options: To preserve realistic pressure, usable evidence, and repeatable feedback rather than turning practice into casual brainstorming.
To prevent the interviewer from asking follow-up questions.
To maximize the number of prompts attempted per hour regardless of depth.
To avoid keeping any written notes after the session.
questions.explanation: A good workflow raises the signal quality of each session.

questions.question: Which stage is most dangerous to skip after the live mock ends?
options: The feedback and debrief stages that convert the session into a next action.
The original prompt selection.
The timer setup.
The title of the architecture note.
questions.explanation: Without feedback and debrief, the mock often fails to change future behavior.

questions.question: Why should a mock agenda reserve time for changed constraints or redesign follow-ups?
options: That is often where senior-level prioritization and adaptability become visible.
Because the original prompt is usually too easy to discuss directly.
Because redesign removes the need for a high-level architecture.
Because it guarantees the interviewer will provide ideal hints.
questions.explanation: Follow-ups reveal how well the candidate can reason under changing assumptions.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with a fixed stage agenda for mocks?
options: It improves comparability and discipline, but can feel less spontaneous than a fully free-form conversation.
It lowers all feedback quality while increasing flexibility.
It removes the need for an interviewer role.
It makes 20-minute drills impossible.
questions.explanation: Structure adds value, but it should not become robotic.

questions.question: Which failure mode most often degrades pair mocks?
options: The interviewer rescues the candidate too early, so the session measures collaboration more than interview performance.
The note template has too many markdown headings.
The whiteboard uses simple rectangles.
The candidate estimates peak QPS before average QPS.
questions.explanation: Well-intentioned help can erase the main signal you wanted from the mock.

questions.question: Which observability signal best indicates the mock workflow itself is healthy?
options: The percentage of sessions that reach feedback, debrief, and at least one concrete next action.
The number of prompts listed in the scenario bank.
The average number of services drawn per mock.
The total length of the candidate's final answer.
questions.explanation: A healthy workflow closes the loop consistently.

questions.question: If you only have 20 minutes for a drill, what should stay intact?
options: The full set of high-signal stages, but with compressed timing and a narrower prompt scope.
Only the architecture deep dive, because clarification and feedback are optional.
Only the debrief, because the design itself can be skipped.
A long pre-brief, because live pressure matters less.
questions.explanation: The same loop still matters in short drills; the depth changes, not the principles.

questions.question: How should a solo mock compensate for the absence of a live interviewer?
options: By predefining timing, interruption points, and feedback prompts so the session still produces evidence.
By removing follow-up questions entirely.
By answering with polished written prose only.
By skipping the mistake log because no external reviewer was present.
questions.explanation: Solo mocks need synthetic structure to preserve interview-like constraints.
